metadata.title: Eigenvectors: the directions a matrix can't turn
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Quantum Mechanics Vol. 5 candidate 03. Source: M-08 (Eigenvalues and Diagonalization), The Eigenvalue Problem: Invariant Directions.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL=eigenvectors (invariant directions, the true/kept thing); CRIMSON=all other vectors that rotate off-axis
metadata.source: Vol. 5, M-08 (Eigenvalues and Diagonalization), The Eigenvalue Problem: Invariant Directions
metadata.note: Exclusions: do not compute a characteristic polynomial on screen — this is the picture, not the algebra.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B12: palette=claude but the outro is 'OutroCTA' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A matrix acts on a vector and rotates it. Every arrow in the plane points somewhere new. Almost every arrow.

card.dek: Eigenvectors: the directions a matrix can't turn

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. A matrix acts on a vector and rotates it. Every arrow in the plane points somewhere new. Almost every arrow.

beats.narration_text: Apply a linear map to a fan of arrows and watch them scatter to new angles. But two directions come out pointing exactly where they went in. Why does every matrix have these special directions?

card.dek: Every matrix rotates most arrows — but some it can only stretch. Why must those directions exist?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Apply a linear map to a fan of arrows and watch them scatter to new angles. But two directions come out pointing exactly where they went in. Why does every matr

beats.narration_text: Take an arbitrary matrix. Apply it to a ring of unit arrows. Watch where they land — they fan out to new directions, rotated and scaled by different amounts.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Take an arbitrary matrix. Apply it to a ring of unit arrows. Watch where they land — they fan out to new directions, rotated and scaled by different amounts.

beats.narration_text: Most arrows tilt after the transformation. The question is whether any arrow stays on its own line — stretched but not rotated. That arrow would satisfy A times v equals lambda times v.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Most arrows tilt after the transformation. The question is whether any arrow stays on its own line — stretched but not rotated. That arrow would satisfy A times

beats.narration_text: The eigenvectors are exactly those arrows. Watch the transformation: a general arrow fans to a new angle, but the eigenvectors slide only along their own line.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The eigenvectors are exactly those arrows. Watch the transformation: a general arrow fans to a new angle, but the eigenvectors slide only along their own line.

beats.narration_text: Each eigenvector has a corresponding eigenvalue — the stretch factor. A negative eigenvalue means the arrow flips and stretches. It still stays on its own line.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Each eigenvector has a corresponding eigenvalue — the stretch factor. A negative eigenvalue means the arrow flips and stretches. It still stays on its own line.

beats.narration_text: In quantum mechanics, measuring an observable projects the system into an eigenstate. The eigenvalue is the measurement outcome — the number you read off the detector.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In quantum mechanics, measuring an observable projects the system into an eigenstate. The eigenvalue is the measurement outcome — the number you read off the de

beats.narration_text: Energy levels, quantum numbers, spin outcomes — all of quantum mechanics is built on finding the invariant directions of the right operator.

props.headline: Energy levels, quantum numbers, spin outcomes — all of quantum mechanics…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Energy levels, quantum numbers, spin outcomes — all of quantum mechanics is built on finding the invariant directions of the right operator.

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: a two-by-two matrix with eigenvalues 3 and minus 1. Apply it to a fan of eight arrows. Six fan away from their starting angles. Two stay on their own lines — one stretched by 3, one flipped and stretched.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Illustrative: a two-by-two matrix with eigenvalues 3 and minus 1. Apply it to a fan of eight arrows. Six fan away from their starting angles. Two stay on their

beats.narration_text: A matrix rotates almost every arrow. The eigenvectors are the exceptions — only stretched, never turned. Every measurement outcome in quantum mechanics is one of these special directions.

card.dek: Eigenvectors: invariant directions, only stretched. Measurement = projection onto one.
